channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: Glen Powell Will Play Fox McCloud In The Super Mario Galaxy Movie
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday, Nintendo announced that Fox McCloud, the protagonist of the kind-of-dead but still very much beloved space flight shooter series Star Fox, will appear in The Super Mario Galaxy Movie. Fans weren’t happy to have the surprise ruined ahead of time, though one thing that remained under wraps was who would be voicing the anthropomorphic fox pilot, which was surprising considering how much Nintendo and Illumination love hyping up the all-star voice casts of these movies. Now we know that Glen Powell, known for his acting in big movies like Top Gun: Maverick and Twisters, will be the voice of Fox in the film.

Powell posted a video on his Instagram account announcing that he would be taking on the role this morning, just about 24 hours after Nintendo and Illumination confirmed the character’s appearance in the film.

Powell joins the stacked cast of newcomers, including Brie Larson as Rosalina, Donald Glover as Yoshi, and Benny Safdie as Bowser Jr. This follows the trend in these movies of picking big celebrity actors to play Nintendo’s characters rather than using the actors who play them in the games. Fox has been played by several actors over the years, with Mike West playing the character in most of his appearances in both Star Fox games and in the most recent Super Smash Bros. games. Though we know Powell is voicing the space pilot in the movie, we have yet to hear his performance in any of the trailers. Maybe Nintendo and Illumination will actually keep that part under wraps until the movie premieres next week on April 1.



https://kotaku.com/super-mario-galaxy-movie-glen-powell-star-fox-mccloud-voice-2000682628
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Internet Reacts To The PlayStation 5’s $100 Price Jump
item.content:encoded:
If you bought a PlayStation 5 in 2020 for the original $400 / $500 price, consider yourself lucky. By the end of next week it will cost at least $150 more. Sony cites “pressures in the global economic landscape” for the decision. How relatable.

For a console generation that has been defined as much by bad news about canceled games and studio closures as it has new hit releases, it’s an especially tough time for Sony to try to justify the move. As sympathetic as one might be to the plight of the modern-day tech company in these unprecedented times, some would argue that Sony hasn’t really offered anything this generation that makes the PS5 so invaluable it feels worth the extra $150 from what it cost in 2020.

So now the internet is reacting with a mix of disbelief at the console maker’s audacity, annoyance at the circumstances that got us here, nostalgia for when consoles actually went down in price, and worry about what this could mean for the price of the PlayStation 6 whenever it launches in 2027 or beyond.

Kevin Butler. $299.

I miss when consoles actually went down in price the longer the generation went on… https://t.co/aG530XWAX4 pic.twitter.com/kU9BWvPWwp

— Radec (@realradec) March 27, 2026

If nothing changes I wouldn't be surprised if the base PS6 launches at $700 or more. pic.twitter.com/6334p0AVac

— Stealth (@Stealth40k) March 27, 2026

2010s: “If millennials stopped spending money on smashed avocado and expensive coffee, they could afford a house”

2020s: “If millennials stopped spending money on eggs and gasoline, they could afford a PS5” https://t.co/LZw1BAPPwZ

— Daniel Ahmad (@ZhugeEX) March 27, 2026

On the one hand, tariffs and the global economy are making prices go up for everything

On the other hand, who the hell in heaven and earth would buy one of these in 2026 https://t.co/SpeWaTMf10

— Paul Tassi (@PaulTassi) March 27, 2026

$650…..for a base PS5 https://t.co/4m3fU2DL1r pic.twitter.com/mW9MlHQPgn

— TheNCSmaster (@TheNCSmaster) March 27, 2026

Consoles used to get cheaper as they got older, and the PS5 is coming up on six years old this November. Sony even said the console was nearing the “latter stage of its life cycle” back in 2024, and it doesn’t have a whole lot to show for it beyond Sony’s several failed live-service games, a bloody trail of dead studios, and Astro Bot, a game about celebrating the PlayStation legacy that is conspicuously lacking in characters from PS5 games.

And with reports that Sony is moving away from PC ports, great games like Ghost of Yotei and promising upcoming releases like Saros and Marvel’s Wolverine won’t be playable on anything but a PS5. Now if you want in on this hot mess, you gotta pay $600 at a minimum. Not a particularly compelling sales pitch, Sony. Especially if those same people will have to consider the cost of upgrading to a PS6 in just the next few years. And what will that cost. $1,000?



https://kotaku.com/ps5-price-reactions-playstation-6-pro-ai-shortage-2000682621
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Expanse: Osiris Reborn Devs Admit Generative AI Is Being Used But Claim Everything Is Still ‘100 Percent Human Made’
item.content:encoded:
The Expanse: Osiris Reborn, a very Mass Effect-esque video game based on James S. A. Corey’s popular book series and developed by Warhammer 40,000: Rogue Trader maker Owlcat Games, got a brand new trailer and a rough release window announcement (spring 2027) during yesterday’s Xbox Partner Preview showcase. However, as promising as the game looks, the only thing anyone seems to be talking about today is Owlcat Games’ confirmation that generative AI is being used during The Expanse: Osiris Reborn’s development.

As revealed by Eurogamer, who quizzed Owlcat Games’ PR manager prior to yesterday’s Xbox Partner Preview showcase, while generative AI tools will be leaned upon during development, the end result will still be “human made.” “We don’t use it to create any assets that will be in the game,” PR manager Katharina Popp said. “We use it a lot for prototyping, trying things out, placeholders. They will all be replaced at the end…We use it basically for trying out things on a technical level.”

“For example, looking how a 2D image looks in 3D, or changing colors to what looks good,” Popp continued. “So it’s basically for being able to iterate faster. But we don’t use it to write, we don’t use AI voice actors, so everything that will be in the final version will definitely 100 percent be human made.”

Eurogamer notes in their article that Owlcat Games already had to clarify its stance on generative AI tools back in March 2024 after it posted a concept artist job listing that required interviewees to have expertise with “concept generation using AI and other modern tools.”

Of course, as Crimson Desert proved, even if generative AI is (ostensibly) only used for stuff like placeholders and prototyping, some of that slop still has a funny habit of ending up in the full release. My own personal distaste for AI-generated content aside, I feel like saying artwork will be fully “human made” even if it’s based on an AI-generated iteration remains a bit of a grey area. You can alter it and change it as much as you like, but the influence will always be there, whether the player is aware and cares about it or not.



https://kotaku.com/expanse-osiris-genai-sci-fi-rpg-mass-effect-owlcat-2000682315
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
